main_summary.summary: Lisa Anderson Todd, interviewed June 24, 2013 at her home in Washington, D.C. by Emilye Crosby of the Southern Oral History Program for the Civil Rights History Project, recounts her path from a suburban New Jersey upbringing and Cornell undergraduate years into Mississippi Freedom Summer 1964. In summer 1963 she joined an Ecumenical Voluntary Service work camp at Tougaloo College and a follow-on American Friends Service Committee voter-registration project in Greensboro, North Carolina, meeting Fannie Lou Hamer in Ruleville and Bob Moses, Jane Stembridge, Memphis Norman, Rev. Ed King, and John Salter Jr. at Tougaloo. In 1964 she canvassed for the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party in Greenville under SNCC project director Charles E. Cobb Jr., working with Pam Trotman, Pat Vail, Muriel Tillinghast, and host family Jerry and Lamar Britton. She attended the MFDP challenge at the Democratic National Convention in Atlantic City and entered Stanford Law School that fall, later clerking for the National Lawyers Guild with Claudia Shropshire Morcom in summer 1965. Todd retired in 2006 to research and write a book on the MFDP convention challenge, drawing on her preserved diary, letters, the Anne Romaine oral histories, and the LBJ tapes.
key_themes: Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party convention challenge
Freedom Summer voter registration in Greenville
white volunteer experience and SNCC organizing
1963 Tougaloo work camp and Greensboro AFSC project
memory, archival research, and historical correction
main_summary.historical_significance:
Todd's testimony is distinctive for two reasons. First, she came to Freedom Summer with prior 1963 grounding in Mississippi (the Tougaloo Ecumenical Voluntary Service work camp) and Greensboro (an AFSC voter registration project that registered 803 people), which positioned her as an early SNCC applicant and gave her direct contact with Fannie Lou Hamer in Ruleville before Hamer's national prominence. She offers ground-level detail on the Washington County MFDP delegate-selection process and the friction between SNCC organizers and established Black Democratic Party operatives such as the local figure she identifies as Mr. Chappell.

Second, Todd is a participant-researcher: from 2006 onward she has spent years reconstructing the Atlantic City credentials-committee fight using the Anne Romaine oral histories, the LBJ telephone-tape archive, and the historiography around Joseph L. Rauh Jr., Walter Reuther, Hubert Humphrey, Bayard Rustin, Bob Moses, Courtland Cox, and Rep. Charles Diggs Jr. Her account explicitly distinguishes contemporaneous memory from later-acquired knowledge, names points where standard narratives (e.g., that LBJ's televised press conference was 'hastily called' to preempt Hamer's testimony) appear to rest on a single secondary source, and adds detail on the Reuther-Mondale Tuesday manipulation and the simultaneous two-room arrangement that produced the symbolic two-seat offer.

chapters.title: Suburban New Jersey Beginnings
chapters.summary: Todd describes her childhood as the daughter of a corporate lawyer father (Carl Anderson) and a psychiatric social worker mother who grew up poor in Hibbing, Minnesota. She characterizes her household as moderate Republican but oriented toward public service, with no direct exposure to local Black residents in Summit, New Jersey.

chapters.title: 1963 Tougaloo Work Camp
chapters.summary: Todd recounts the Ecumenical Voluntary Service work camp at Tougaloo College in summer 1963, including nonviolence workshops, speakers at the Social Science Lab, and a visit to the SNCC office in Greenwood at Bob Moses's invitation. She meets Fannie Lou Hamer in Ruleville, hears the account of the Winona beating, and attends a mass meeting at Williams Chapel Church.

chapters.title: Greensboro AFSC Project
chapters.summary: Todd describes the second 1963 work camp, an American Friends Service Committee voter-registration project in Greensboro, North Carolina, where 18 volunteers lived in a church basement and registered 803 new voters in cooperation with local registrars. She contrasts the relative freedom of Greensboro with the surveillance and danger of Mississippi.

chapters.title: Tougaloo Speakers and Jackson Context
chapters.summary: Todd recalls nonviolence workshops at Tougaloo, meeting Dave Dennis of CORE, Memphis Norman, Rev. Ed King, Jane Schutt, Timothy Jenkins, and Allan Knight Chalmers. She describes the post-Evers shift in Jackson from direct action to voter registration, the NAACP's decision to stop posting bail, and the tension between national NAACP and the local North Jackson youth branch working with John Salter Jr. and Rev. Ed King.

chapters.title: Cornell and the Civil Rights Act
chapters.summary: Todd describes returning to Cornell for her senior year transformed by the 1963 summer. She works with Cornell United Religious Work under Paul Gibbons to lobby for passage of the Civil Rights Act, takes Robin M. Williams Jr.'s race-relations sociology course, joins a sorority that pledged Jewish students against national policy, and travels to Washington over spring vacation to observe Senate debate.

chapters.title: Applying to Freedom Summer
chapters.summary: Todd applies to SNCC in fall 1963 after correspondence with Walter Tillow, is accepted without an interview based on her 1963 record, and arrives late at the Oxford, Ohio orientation following a friend's wedding. She recalls Bob Moses's overalls-and-t-shirt address, Vincent Harding's remarks, Bruce Hanson on internalized prejudice, and Carl Fleming of Newsweek participating in nonviolence role-play.
related_events: disappearance of Chaney, Schwerner, and Goodman

chapters.title: Greenville Canvassing and Freedom Registration
chapters.summary: Todd is assigned to Greenville, Mississippi for voter registration under project directors George Rawick (first month) and then Muriel Tillinghast, while believing Charles E. Cobb Jr. was project director for Washington, Issaquena, and Sharkey counties. She lives first with Mrs. Harden in the south end and then with drugstore owners Jerry and Lamar Britton on Nelson Street. She canvasses with Pam Trotman and local men Charles Moore and James Williams, collecting MFDP Freedom Registration signatures.

chapters.title: MFDP Delegate Selection in Washington County
chapters.summary: Todd organizes a precinct meeting and attends the Washington County MFDP convention, where she watches local Democratic Party operative Mr. Chappell maneuver to get traditional leaders elected as county delegates. She attends the second congressional district caucus in Greenville, where Fannie Lou Hamer led the singing at Friendship Baptist Church, but is not present at the state convention in Jackson.

chapters.title: Movement Discussions and the Hodding Carter Pool
chapters.summary: Todd describes growing internal tensions: SNCC staff who voiced anger and questioned nonviolence, two demonstrations at the federal building for federal protection following the disappearance of the three workers, and arguments over whether COFO Jackson had to approve local demonstrations. She also recounts the project's swim at Hodding Carter's pool, arranged by Dorothy Jones, which ended when neighbors arrived on horseback.

chapters.title: Atlantic City: MFDP Challenge
chapters.summary: Todd and Pat Vail drive to Atlantic City and operate as freelance lobbyists from Union Baptist Temple Church, missing Fannie Lou Hamer's televised credentials-committee testimony but witnessing the Tuesday afternoon meeting after the symbolic two-seat offer. She describes the Tuesday-night delegate vote against the offer, Joseph L. Rauh Jr.'s dual role, Walter Reuther's late arrival and pressure on the Mondale subcommittee, Hubert Humphrey's role, the Diggs list of credentials-committee supporters that was overnight-flipped, and her later research using LBJ tapes and Anne Romaine's oral histories.

chapters.title: Law School and 1965 Mississippi Return
chapters.summary: Todd returns to New Jersey, helps establish the Herbert Lee Memorial Community Center fundraising effort with Dr. Matthew Page and Charles Moore, then drives to Stanford Law School and lives with Karen Davis. After being reassigned away from the Lawyers' Committee for Civil Rights Under Law for sounding too militant, she clerks for the National Lawyers Guild in Tougaloo with Claudia Shropshire Morcom in summer 1965, evaluating Title III public-facilities litigation in Madison County, Greenwood, Greenville, and Jackson.
related_events: Herbert Lee Memorial Community Center founding

chapters.title: Government Contracts Career
chapters.summary: Todd describes her post-1967 career: legal services applications, the United Planning Organization under executive director Wiley A. Branton Sr., private practice with Trovon Bower and Morgan, Lewis & Bockius, and selection as an administrative judge on the NASA Board of Contract Appeals after a three-hour interview. She characterizes leaving UPO in 1969 as a response to the changed atmosphere of Black Power and her own assessment of where she could contribute.
related_events: late-1960s Black Power transition in DC

chapters.title: Retirement Research and Reflection
chapters.summary: Todd describes her seven-year project, begun on retirement in April 2006, to write the MFDP convention-challenge book that became this interview's framing concern. She names her sources (Anne Romaine's UNC oral histories, LBJ tapes at the Texas LBJ Library, John Dittmer's footnoting advice, Mary King's mentoring), reflects on distinguishing memory from research, and closes with thoughts about young people, voting, and the long arc from Freedom Summer to Obama.
